hints.present imperative: Imperative singular of 'regere' would be 'rege' — 'regere' is the infinitive
hints.future infinitive: Future infinitive would be 'recturum esse' — 'regere' is present infinitive
hints.perfect infinitive: Perfect infinitive would be 'rexisse' — 'regere' is present infinitive

hints.rogo, rogare, rogavi, rogatum: These are the parts of 'rogare' (to ask)
hints.refero, referre, rettuli, relatum: These are the parts of 'referre' (to carry back/report)
hints.regno, regnare, regnavi, regnatum: These are the parts of 'regnare' (to reign) — different verb

steps.reveal: imperio = with command/authority (abl. sg., ablative of means)

hints.dative: While 'imperio' could be dative, here it is ablative of means — rule 'by' authority
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'imperii' — 'imperio' is ablative of means

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'imperii' ends in -ii (2nd declension neuter)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'imperii' ends in -ii
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'imperii' ends in -ii

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'populi' — 'populos' with -os is accusative plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'populis' — 'populos' is accusative
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'populis' — 'populos' is accusative, object of 'regere'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'populi' ends in -i (2nd declension)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'populi' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'populi' ends in -i

steps.reveal: Romane = O Roman (voc. sg., direct address)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Romanus' — the -e ending marks vocative of 2nd decl. -us words
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Romano' — 'Romane' with -e is vocative
hints.adverb: 'Romane' is not an adverb here — it is vocative singular, addressing the Roman

hints.to lie: That would be 'mentiri' — 'memento' comes from 'meminisse' (to remember)
hints.to fear: That would be 'metuere' — 'memento' comes from 'meminisse' (to remember)
hints.to deserve: That would be 'merere' — 'memento' comes from 'meminisse' (to remember)

steps.reveal: memento = remember! (fut. imperative of defective verb 'meminisse')

hints.present imperative: The present imperative is rare for 'meminisse' — 'memento' is the future imperative
hints.present indicative: 'Memento' is not indicative — it is the future imperative, a solemn command
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'meminerim' — 'memento' is future imperative

hints.moneo, monere, monui, monitum: These are the parts of 'monere' (to warn/advise)
hints.mentior, mentiri, mentitus sum: These are the parts of 'mentiri' (to lie/deceive)
hints.memoro, memorare, memoravi, memoratum: These are the parts of 'memorare' (to recount/mention)

translation: You, O Roman, remember to rule the peoples with authority
